blockdesc-blastfurnacedoor*: The iron door of the blast furnace.
blockdesc-hopperreinforced*: Combines crushed ores into blast mix for the bell hopper below.
blockdesc-hopperbell*: Drips stockpiled blast mix down into the furnace shaft.
blockdesc-blastfurnace-tuyere*: A hot blast intake for the furnace shaft.
blockdesc-blastfurnacetap*: Drains the furnace's molten reservoir into a canal below.
blockdesc-slag*: A block of solidified slag from the blast furnace.
blockdesc-slagpath*: A sturdy road paved with crushed blast furnace slag.
blockdesc-solidifiediron*: Iron that cooled and hardened before it could be tapped or poured.
blockdesc-cowperstove-intake*: The intake face of a cowper stove.
blockdesc-smokestack-intake*: Vents surplus furnace exhaust to the sky.
blockdesc-moltencanal-start*: The anchor of a molten canal network.
blockdesc-moltencanal-bend*: A 90° bend in a molten canal run.
blockdesc-moltencanal-tap*: Pours metal out of the far end of a canal.
blockdesc-moltencanal-moldpedestal*: Fills a mold it from the canal network.
blockdesc-moltenbarrel*: Bulk storage for liquid metal or slag.
blockdesc-convertercontrol*: Used to operate the bessemer converter.
blockdesc-converterbessemer*: Massive vessel that blows molten iron into steel.
blockdesc-convertertransmission*: Uses axle's mechanical power to rotate the vessesl.
itemdesc-slag*: A by-product tapped from the blast furnace.
itemdesc-powderedslag*: Ground slag, future mortar or fertilizer.
bessemer-err-incomplete-count: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
bessemer-err-notbuilt: The converter vessel is not finished being built.
bessemer-err-nopower: No mechanical power! Connect an axle to the transmission.
bessemer-err-intake-misaligned: The gas intake must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-info-intake-misaligned: Gas intake misaligned! It must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-err-transmission-misaligned: The transmission must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-info-transmission-misaligned: Transmission misaligned! It must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-err-converter-present: The converter vessel is already in place.
bessemer-err-converter-blocked: The converter cannot be placed! Clear the 3x3x3 space for it first.
bessemer-err-materials: Needs {0} large gear and {1} iron/steel rods in your hotbar.
bf-info-doorclosed: Door must be closed for firing!
bf-info-exhaustfull: Exhaust network is full! Production halted.
bf-info-needsmix: Blast Furnace needs more blast mix to fire!
bf-info-partiallylit: Piles are partially lit. Waiting for the fire to spread...
bf-info-ready: Blast Furnace is ready! Ignite the blast mix inside and close the door.
bf-error-incomplete: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
structure-incomplete-count: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
tap-err-nocanal: No canal start found below the tap!
bessemer-status-solidified: To much solidified residue! Break the converter to clear it
bessemer-status-chiselout: Solidified residue - chisel it out from the upper hatch
bessemer-status-coolingtochisel: Residue cooling - wait for it to harden, then chisel it out from the upper hatch
bessemer-status-pouring-full: Output full or metal type mismatch!
cowperstove-status-exhaustmix: Cannot heat up, exhaust mixes with the air!
game:ingameerror-smex-moltenspill: The molten metal spilled out of the mold!
game:ingameerror-smex-needemptyhands: The metal is still liquid! Take the mold with an empty hand.
game:ingameerror-smex-moldtoosmall: This mold is used with the mold pedestal.
game:ingameerror-smex-moldtoolarge: This mold will not fit on the pedestal.
game:ingameerror-smex-canaltoohot: Wait for the metal to harden!
game:ingameerror-smex-bessemertoohot: The residue is still too hot - wait for it to harden!
game:ingameerror-smex-bessemertoofull: Too much metal to chisel out - break the converter to salvage materials.
game:ingameerror-smex-canalnotempty: Cannot seal while there is metal!
game:ingameerror-smex-molddisabled: This mold is disabled on this server.
command-molds-desc: Enable or disable the mod's added casting molds (plate, ingot, rod). Recipe and handbook changes apply on the next world reload; already-placed molds stop casting immediately.
command-molds-set: {0} mold {1}. Recipe and handbook changes apply on the next world reload; placed molds have already stopped casting.
command-steel-desc: Show or set the steelmaking recipe cost level (normal/cheap). Per-recipe numbers are in smex_recipes.json; takes effect on the next world reload.
command-steel-set: Steel recipe costs set to {0}. Reload the world to apply.
advancedsteelmaking-text:
Steelmaking Expanded adds a full industrial pipeline for turning out iron and steel in bulk, and it works in two stages. In Stage I you build a Blast Furnace and the support machinery around it to smelt iron, and in Stage II you bolt a Bessemer Converter onto that line to refine the molten iron into steel. Build the iron stage first and get it running reliably, because the steel stage is expensive and feeds directly from it. Be warned that the blast furnace is built from tier-3 refractory bricks, so you must already have reached the steel age to attempt it, and none of this machinery runs without steam power - have a steam setup working before you start.

The full chain is spread across four companion articles. The Blast Furnace is the heart of the operation and where Stage I begins; Hot Blast & Gas Handling covers the cowper stoves, the smoke stack and how everything ties into the pipe network, without which the furnace will never reach iron's melting point; Molten Metal & Casting explains the canals, taps and molds that turn the melt into finished items and where to route surplus metal or waste slag; and The Bessemer Converter opens Stage II, the heavy machinery that blows iron into steel and draws on every mechanic before it. The pipes, boilers and engines that power all of this come from the companion mod - see Steam Power - and are used both to supply mechanical power and to pressurise the air the furnace breathes.

In brief, a full iron run goes like this. You stock the Reinforced Hopper of the furnace with crushed iron ore, crushed coke and lime and let its Bell Hopper drip the resulting blast mix down until the shaft is full, then set that mix alight with a torch and shut the door. A powered Air Blower on a steam engine pushes air through a heated Cowper Stove and in through the Tuyeres as hot pressurised air, while the Smoke Stack vents the excess gases. You keep the internal temperature climbing past about 1482 °C into the melting phase, alternating between two cowper stoves so the incoming air never goes cold, and once iron and slag have pooled you open the Molten Metal Taps to drain them down the canals into molds or barrels before they have a chance to solidify. Keep the hopper fed, the exhaust flowing and the boilers boiling, and repeat.
handbook-blastfurnace-text:
The Blast Furnace is the heart of the whole operation: a tall multiblock built mostly from refractory brick, with an iron door, two tuyeres (air intakes) low on the shaft and two gas outlets high up. Place the door block first, as it is the control block, then hold shift + rightmouse on it to see the build outline and fill in every highlighted block until the structure reports itself complete. All told it needs 98 tier-3 refractory bricks, the door, two tuyeres, two molten metal taps, two tier-3 refractory gas outlets, a reinforced hopper and a bell hopper; the hollow shaft inside is left empty for the hoppers to fill.

The furnace does not eat ore directly - it burns blast mix, a packed pile of ore and fuel that you cannot craft by hand. Instead it is made by the pair of hoppers stacked above the shaft. The Reinforced Hopper is the magazine: open it with rightmouse and load three ingredients - crushed iron ore, crushed coke and lime as flux - and it combines twelve crushed iron, three crushed coke and one lime into sixteen blast mix, stockpiling up to sixty units of iron internally. You crush iron ore and coke in a pulverizer, bake coke from coal in a coke oven, and can even crush scrap iron bits back into crushed iron for resmelting. Below it sits the Bell Hopper, which drips that stockpile down into the shaft; toggle the dropping with ctrl + rightmouse on either hopper, and it stops on its own once the furnace is full. Should a fuel pile ever break loose, note that blast mix behaves exactly like a coal pile - you can rightmouse the ground to lay it back down, stack more onto it and feed it back through the hoppers, and it will even burn as ordinary fuel in a pinch, though not for much.

To fire the furnace, let the bell hopper fill the shaft, set the blast mix alight and close the door. Once every pile in the hearth is burning, the door is shut and the exhaust outlets are clear, the furnace begins to climb toward about 1400 °C on its own; but to melt iron you must hold it above iron's melting point of roughly 1482 °C for several minutes, which takes the hot pressurised air described in the Hot Blast article, at which point it enters the melting phase and converts the blast mix into a reservoir of molten iron and molten slag.

That fire is fragile. Opening the door, running out of blast mix, a choked or backed-up exhaust, or letting the molten reservoir overflow will all snuff it out, and an extinguished melt solidifies in the hearth into iron blocks you have to mine back out. Keep the exhaust flowing and the door shut while it works the iron.
handbook-hotblast-text:
Hot blast is preheated, pressurised air, and it is what lets the furnace climb hot enough to actually melt iron. A bare blast furnace tops out around 1400 °C, short of iron's melting point; to push past that you reclaim the heat the furnace throws away in its exhaust and feed it back in through the air. For this to work the air you supply must be both pressurised above about 2.5 atm and heated above roughly 800 °C.

Everything breathes through pipes from Pipes and Power Expanded, and here a network carries one of two gases: ordinary air, and the hot exhaust that spills from the furnace. A handful of machines move it around. The Air Blower is a steam-engine sub-machine that pressurises air and forces it through the cowper stoves and into the furnace tuyeres. Valves open and close a line by hand, which you will need constantly for swapping the cowper stoves over and steering flow through the network, while pressure valves open by themselves above their gate pressure to spill overflow to another side (their input side wears a copper-textured rim). Outlets connect a network to a structure's face and form part of its multiblock footprint. Each network tracks its gas type, volume and temperature, and any open-ended pipe leaks, so plan how many machines you run carefully to avoid under- or over-supplying.

The Cowper Stove is a heat regenerator, and it is what makes the air hot. Pipe the furnace's exhaust into a stove and it soaks that heat into the heatsink at its core - "charging" - which you can speed up by burning a coal pile inside, fastest of all with anthracite; just beware that burning low grades of coal makes the stove itself give off exhaust from its main output. Once charged, route plain pressurised air through the same stove and it pours out scorching hot, and fed into the tuyeres it drives the furnace's internal temperature back up toward about 1700 °C. Each stove needs 48 refractory bricks of any tier, a cowper stove intake as its control block, four heat sinks, two refractory gas outlets, one refractory gas passthrough and an iron hatch door, with one slot below the intake left open for the coal pile that speeds the exchange.

A single stove can only do one job at a time, and a charged core runs down as you draw heat from it, so you run two stoves as a pair: while one charges on exhaust, the other discharges hot air to the furnace, and when the discharging one drops below 800 °C you swap their roles with valves. Keep exhaust and air on separate paths, because if they mix inside a stove it cannot heat up.

A blast furnace also makes more exhaust than the stoves can swallow, and a backed-up exhaust line will choke and extinguish it, so the Smoke Stack vents the surplus to the sky and acts as the safety valve of the whole system. It needs a smoke stack intake as its control block, 24 refractory bricks of any tier and 36 of any ordinary brick. Above all, keep an eye on the stoves' temperature - let them cool and the furnace will stop smelting or go out entirely.
handbook-casting-text:
Molten metal is plumbed, not carried. You lay a Molten Canal (Start) as the anchor of a network and run straights, bends and junctions out from it, all crafted from cobblestone (or burned brick for a tidier look) and fire clay. The furnace is drained through its Molten Metal Taps - the lower tap pours iron and the upper one pours slag - and each tap needs the start of a canal beneath it before it will let you toggle it with rightmouse. At the far end a Canal Tap pours into a barrel, useful when you have excess to drain; bear in mind that molten iron actually flows through the canals as a simulation, so not everything will necessarily drain.

An empty straight canal can be sealed by hand to act as a manual valve: rightmouse it with four fire clay and it caps over, severing the network at that block, and a chisel reopens it and recovers two of the clay. Only a drained canal can be sealed, but it is a handy way to choose which path the metal takes when several are open to it.

Watch the temperature throughout, because metal in the canals cools slowly and will solidify in place if it drops too far. A hardened canal stops flowing and must be chipped clear; once it has cooled enough, a chisel and hammer recover most of the metal as bits, which can themselves be crushed back into crushed iron for the furnace. Keep your runs short, keep them fed, and empty them before they - or the furnace - go cold.

Casting itself happens at two kinds of destination. A Mold Pedestal casts small tool molds: shift + rightmouse places or removes the mold and ctrl + rightmouse toggles the pour. This mod also adds larger molds - a plate mold, a quad rod mold and a double ingot mold - which you clay-form and fire like any tool mold, while the big anvil and helve-hammer molds go directly under the canal tap instead. The other destination is a Molten Barrel, bulk storage for liquid metal that you can use to buffer excess iron or to hold slag from the top tap; when its contents solidify you can chisel them out just like a canal, as long as you set the barrel on the ground first.

Freshly filled molds are dangerously hot, and carrying one bare-handed will burn you, so wear heavy leather or a blacksmith's gloves. While the metal is still liquid the mold can only ride in your empty active hand - stashing it in a bag or on a rack spills the metal at once - so set it down, let it harden, and then collect the casting with a plain rightmouse.

Finally, slag is not just waste. Solidified slag grinds into powdered slag, which mixed with slaked lime in a barrel yields mortar, and spread over farmland acts as a phosphate fertilizer - a furnace byproduct turned into building stock and crop yield.
handbook-bessemer-text:
The Bessemer process is how you turn raw iron into steel. Raw iron carries too much contamination - excess carbon, sulfur and other elements - and the idea is to literally boil the iron by blowing air through it, burning those impurities out and purifying it into mild steel. The Bessemer Converter is the machine that does it, taking molten iron and blowing it into steel. It is the most demanding machine in the mod - it draws both mechanical power and a supply of pressurised air, and only works as the final stage of an already-running molten line - so do not attempt it until your iron tier is well established.

The converter is a 3x3x3 vessel raised through right-click construction. Place its control block first, then rightmouse it with one large gear and eight iron or steel rods in your hotbar to start the vessel, and build it up through its stages until it reports complete. Around the vessel sit its service ports - a gas intake, a mechanical transmission, an input tap, and an output canal-start block. Beyond the control block itself you will need the converter (raised in place from the gear and rods), a Bessemer transmission, a Bessemer gas intake, and a short run of molten canal: one start, two straights and a tap. The vessel's own construction stages consume, all together, 40 iron or steel plates, 42 nails-and-strips, 18 rods, 84 tier-3 refractory bricks, 84 fire clay and three straight gas pipes.

Powering it draws on two services from the systems you have already built. For mechanical power, connect an axle to the transmission so the vessel can tilt and blow - a steam engine is recommended here for the torque it needs. For air, pipe pressurised air from a blower into the converter's gas intake, which must face the same way as the control block or it will not couple. Then run a molten canal from a furnace tap into the converter's input tap, and a second canal beneath it will carry the finished output toward your molds.

To operate it, set the converter to Filling and pour molten iron in from the canal until it holds as much as you want (it need not be full to run). Switch it to Normal, and with power and air both flowing it refines the charge at about 1800 °C for roughly five minutes into steel. Finally set it to Pouring to drain the finished steel into the output canal, from where it travels the molten network exactly like iron.

The same temperature rule applies inside the vessel: if the charge cools and solidifies before you pour it, you must break the converter with a steel pickaxe to recover the metal and then rebuild, so make sure you can drain it in time. One last note - a single blast furnace produces more iron than one converter can consume, so you will want at least two converters running for continuous steel. All of this machinery takes a great deal of space both horizontally and vertically, and you should make sure every valve and control is easy to reach, because time spent struggling to get at something is time your metal spends solidifying or your furnace spends cooling. This may finally be the moment you build the factory you always wanted.
